Front: A 35-year-old construction worker is brought to the emergency department after falling approximately 10 feet from scaffolding and landing on his feet with his right leg extended and abducted. He immediately felt severe pain in the groin and hip and was unable to walk or bear weight. He denies head trauma or loss of consciousness. He has no prior orthopedic history and is otherwise healthy. No medications or substance use are reported. On physical examination, he is alert and hemodynamically stable. The right lower extremity appears shortened and is held in a position of abduction and external rotation at the hip. There is no swelling or deformity of the thigh. Distal pulses are intact and equal bilaterally. Sensation and motor strength are preserved distally in the affected limb. There is tenderness over the anterior hip, and passive movement of the joint elicits pain. Radiographs are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Back:
Anterior hip dislocation presents with a shortened, externally rotated, and abducted leg after trauma. It occurs less frequently than posterior dislocation but should be suspected after extension-abduction injuries.

Correct answer letter: A

Front: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling off a bunk bed and striking his head on the hardwood floor from a height of approximately 6 feet. His mother reports that he briefly lost consciousness but awoke within a few minutes and has since been drowsy and minimally responsive. On examination, his Glasgow Coma Scale score is 10. He has bilateral periorbital ecchymoses and retroauricular bruising behind the left ear. Clear fluid is observed draining from his left nostril and right ear. His vital signs are: temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure 152/96 mm Hg, heart rate 48/min, and respiratory rate 8/min. Noncontrast CT of the head is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Back:
Basilar skull fractures often present with Battle sign, raccoon eyes, and CSF rhinorrhea or otorrhea. Associated Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) suggests increased ICP. Diagnosis is largely clinical, but CT is confirmatory.

Correct answer letter: A

Front: A 22-year-old man comes to clinic for a routine check-up before traveling abroad. He has no complaints. He is a healthy college student who recently began competing in local strongman events. For the past several months, he has followed a structured training program focused on short, high-intensity sets involving maximal effort lifting, with exercises such as weighted sled pushes, deadlifts, and farmer’s carries. He performs very little aerobic activity and does not participate in endurance sports. On physical exam, he is normotensive, with a muscular build and no abnormalities noted. His BMI is 26.1 kg/m². He enrolls in a study examining muscle adaptations in athletes. A quadriceps biopsy is taken and examined. Compared to sedentary controls, his sample shows adaptation consistent with resistance training. Which of the following best describes the expected histologic finding in this athlete?
Back:
Skeletal muscle hypertrophy is the primary adaptation to anaerobic resistance training. Fiber number remains constant; growth occurs via increased cross-sectional area of existing fibers. Aerobic training (not shown here) leads to increased mitochondria, capillaries, and oxidative enzymes. 4.1 A.Deep peroneal nerve B.Femoral nerve C.Lateral plantar nerve D.Superficial peroneal nerve E.Sural nerve AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Lower limb nerves)SystemNoneTopic

Front: A 62-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic for evaluation of a chronic wound on the bottom of his right foot. He first noticed it approximately three weeks ago when his wife pointed out blood on his sock, although he himself had not felt any pain. He reports that for the past few years, he’s had a “tingly” or “numb” feeling in both feet, especially at night when lying in bed. Occasionally, this sensation disrupts his sleep. He denies fever, chills, or drainage from the wound. The lesion does not interfere with his ability to walk, but he mentions that over the last few months, he has had difficulty feeling the gas pedal while driving. He has no back pain or radiating leg symptoms. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus for 18 years, hypertension, dyslipidemia, coronary artery disease, and peripheral vascular disease. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit five years ago. On further questioning, the patient notes that he is not consistent with checking his feet and has not seen a podiatrist in years. He wears the same pair of shoes daily and admits they are quite worn. His current medications include metformin, insulin glargine, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and aspirin. On review of systems, he denies visual changes, recent falls, bowel or bladder incontinence, and upper extremity numbness. He has not noticed any weight loss or fatigue. His A1c at the last visit was 8.9%, and he has a history of non-healing wounds in the past. He has never had lower extremity amputations. On physical examination, he is afebrile and in no acute distress. There is a 2-cm shallow ulcer with a clean base and surrounding callus on the plantar surface beneath the first metatarsal head of the right foot. There is no erythema, swelling, or fluctuance. A probe-to-bone test is negative. Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation over the plantar surface of both feet, including the toes and heels. There is reduced vibration sense at the great toe bilaterally. Capillary refill is delayed, and dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are diminished on the right. The ankle- brachial index is 0.62 on the affected side. The patient is scheduled for foot imaging and vascular consultation. Which of the following nerves is primarily responsible for sensation to the affected area?
Back:
The lateral plantar nerve, a branch of the tibial nerve, innervates the lateral plantar surface of the foot and is commonly involved in neuropathic ulcers in diabetic patients. Loss of protective sensation in this region predisposes to painless pressure ulcers, particularly under the metatarsal heads. 5.1 A.Abductor digiti minimi B.Extensor carpi radialis longus C.Extensor carpi ulnaris D.Extensor digitorum E.Extensor pollicis longus

Front: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic after injuring his right wrist while playing soccer during lunch break. He recalls running backwards while defending a teammate, tripping over another player's foot, and falling awkwardly onto his outstretched right hand. Since the fall, he has complained of persistent pain at the base of his thumb, especially when twisting a doorknob or picking up objects. His parents report no visible swelling or bruising, but the boy has avoided using his right hand since the injury. On physical exam, his fingers move well and there’s no visible deformity. However, palpation over the anatomical snuffbox on the dorsal radial wrist elicits marked tenderness. Passive radial deviation also causes discomfort. A plain wrist X-ray is obtained and shows no fracture. Strain of which of the following tendons might also be seen in this patient?
Back:
The anatomical snuffbox is bordered by three tendons: abductor pollicis longus, extensor pollicis brevis, and extensor pollicis longus. The extensor pollicis longus tendon forms the medial border and is most susceptible to strain in FOOSH injuries. Even in the absence of fracture, EPL strain should be considered in patients with anatomical snuffbox tenderness.

Front: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department after injuring his right ankle during a recreational soccer match. While attempting to block a shot, he planted his right foot and twisted awkwardly, experiencing a sudden and forceful eversion of the ankle. He immediately felt severe pain and was unable to bear weight. On physical examination, the right ankle is swollen and bruised medially. Palpation along the medial malleolus elicits marked tenderness, and passive eversion of the foot reproduces the pain. The lateral aspect is non-tender, and the anterior drawer test is negative. Neurovascular status is intact. An x-ray of the right ankle is obtained and shown below. Which of the following structures is most likely injured?
Back:
The deltoid ligament stabilizes the medial ankle and is injured with forced eversion. Medial tenderness and increased medial joint space on x-ray are classic findings. Always consider mechanism (eversion vs inversion) and localize tenderness to guide diagnosis.

Front: A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic for follow-up of joint symptoms.Over the past few years, she has experienced intermittent pain and swelling inher hands, wrists, and knees. Laboratory studiesreveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptideantibody, with mild elevation in ESR and CRP. Back:
Smoking is a modifiable risk factor that increases the risk of rheumatoid arthritis flares and reduces treatment efficacy. Smoking cessation should be a key part of RA management. Effective RA control includes both pharmacologic and lifestyle interventions.

Front: A 70-year-old man comes to the outpatient clinic because of a bulge on the lower left side of his abdomen. Front: A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to difficulty using her hands for the past several months. She reports struggling to grasp small objects such as keys and has recently needed assistance with tasks like buttoning her blouse and opening jars. She denies any recent trauma, falls, or fever. Her symptoms have been slowly progressive and worse on the right. She also describes intermittent tingling and dull aching pain that radiates down her right arm, sometimes reaching the hand. She does not report neck stiffness, gait instability, or bowel/bladder incontinence. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both controlled with medication. Neurologic examination shows decreased grip strength and reduced fine motor coordination on the right. Reflexes are symmetric, and no spasticity or hyperreflexia is observed. There is patchy sensory loss in the right hand. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Back:
Cervical foraminal stenosis is caused by nerve root compression at the neural foramina due to degenerative changes. Presents with unilateral weakness, sensory loss, and pain in a dermatomal/radicular distribution without signs of cord compression. MRI of the cervical spine is the diagnostic test of choice to visualize foraminal narrowing and nerve root impingement.

Front: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty moving her right index finger after an accident at home earlier in the morning. She had been rearranging storage boxes on a high shelf in her garage when a plastic container unexpectedly fell. She reflexively reached out with her right hand to catch it, but the corner of the box struck the tip of her outstretched index finger with force. She felt a sharp pain immediately and has since been unable to use the fingertip normally. She denies numbness, tingling, or pain in the other fingers. On examination, the right hand appears normal with no swelling or bruising. There is no tenderness along the metacarpals or carpal bones. The index finger has full passive range of motion, and she can extend it fully. However, when asked to actively bend the fingertip of the index finger, she is unable to flex the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint, while proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal motion remains intact. The rest of the hand functions normally. Which of the following tendons is most likely injured in this patient?
Back:
FDP is the only tendon that flexes the DIP joint; loss of DIP flexion points to FDP injury. FDS flexes the PIP joint; if PIP flexion is intact, FDS is preserved. This case demonstrates classic tendon anatomy tested on USMLE, with presentation and localization guiding the diagnosis.

Front: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling from a tree onto his outstretched right hand. He reports severe pain in the elbow and forearm, along with swelling that developed rapidly after the fall. He has difficulty moving his fingers due to pain. There is no obvious deformity of the forearm or hand, but the elbow appears swollen and tender. Passive extension of the fingers worsens his forearm pain. Radial pulse on the injured side is faint compared to the opposite arm. The fingers are warm but capillary refill is delayed. Sensory testing shows intact sensation over the thenar eminence and dorsal thumb. Radiographs of the elbow are ordered and demonstrate a displaced fracture. Injury to which of the following anatomic locations is most likely responsible for this patient’s vascular compromise?
Back:
A supracondylar fracture of the humerus, most often from a fall on an outstretched hand in children, can injure the brachial artery and median nerve as they pass anterior to the distal humerus. Front: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent right knee pain that has progressively worsened over the past month. He says the pain began after he tripped during basketball practice, but there was no fall or direct trauma. Since then, he has been avoiding sports and complains of intermittent pain that radiates to the thigh. His mother reports that he has been less active recently and appears to be limping, especially at the end of the day. Past medical history is notable for childhood asthma, managed with inhaled corticosteroids, and a learning disability for which he attends special classes. He was born at term by cesarean section due to breech presentation. His diet consists largely of processed foods, and he spends several hours daily playing video games. On examination, his BMI is at the 98th percentile. He walks with an antalgic gait, and there is limited internal rotation of the right hip. Knee examination is unremarkable, and there is no joint effusion or erythema. Passive flexion of the hip elicits discomfort. Neurologic exam is normal. Which of the following is the most significant risk factor for this patient’s condition?
Back:
Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) typically affects obese adolescent boys and presents with progressive hip or referred knee pain, limited hip motion, and an antalgic gait. The most significant risk factor is a high body mass index (BMI), which increases shear stress across the growth plate. Diagnosis is confirmed by imaging, and the definitive management is urgent surgical stabilization with in situ pinning to prevent further slippage and complications such as avascular necrosis.

Front: A 47-year-old woman comes to the outpatient clinic due to pain in her right heel that has been bothering her for the past two months. She says the pain is most noticeable when she first gets out of bed in the morning. After walking for a few minutes, the pain lessens, and she is generally able to go about her day with only mild discomfort. However, the pain often returns if she has been sitting or inactive for long periods and then stands up again. She has not had any recent trauma or systemic symptoms. She works in retail and spends most of her day standing or walking on hard floors. Her BMI is 31 kg/m². On physical examination, there is point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity of the right foot. There is no swelling, erythema, or warmth. Plantar fasciitis causes heel pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning and improves with continued walking. Exam shows point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity with no inflammation or neurologic findings. Front: A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressively worsening bilateral knee pain that began over three years ago. She describes the pain as dull and aching, most noticeable after walking or prolonged standing. She denies morning stiffness, systemic symptoms, or joint redness. Her past medical history includes obesity and venous stasis. On exam, she has crepitus with passive knee motion and visible bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal joints. No erythema or warmth is present. Her BMI is 42 kg/m². Synovial fluid aspirated from the right knee is clear with a WBC count of 950/μL and normal glucose levels. A plain radiograph shows asymmetric joint space narrowing and evidence of subchondral sclerosis. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition?
Back:
Osteoarthritis is a non-inflammatory degenerative joint disease caused by chondrocyte-driven cartilage erosion. The hallmark finding is subchondral eburnation, with joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and sclerosis. Synovial fluid in OA is non-inflammatory (WBC < 2,000/μL), helping distinguish it from RA, gout, or septic arthritis.

Front: A 27-year-old man presents to the clinic after noticing a bump on the back of his right wrist that he first saw two weeks ago. He works in IT support and spends most of the day typing or using a mouse. The lesion has gradually increased in size, but he denies any associated pain, numbness, weakness, redness, or warmth. Front: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after falling off a motorcycle at high speed. He was wearing a helmet and did not lose consciousness. On examination, he has multiple abrasions and a deep laceration over the posterior mid-arm. He is placed in a sling and reports difficulty extending his wrist. Physical exam reveals wrist and finger extension weakness as well as diminished sensation over the dorsum of the right hand and posterior forearm. Elbow flexion and shoulder abduction are preserved. Reflexes are normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
Back:
Injury to the radial nerve along the mid-humerus causes wrist drop and sensory loss over the posterior arm and dorsum of the hand. The nerve’s location in the spiral groove makes it especially vulnerable in midshaft humeral trauma.

Front: A 48-year-old man comes to the emergency department with a swollen,painful left leg. Front: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to right knee pain that has worsened over the past week. He plays as a forward on his school’s soccer team. Back:
Osgood-Schlatter disease is a common cause of anterior knee pain in adolescent athletes due to traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle. It presents with pain and swelling over the tibial tuberosity, especially during activity, and is managed conservatively as it typically resolves with skeletal maturity. 71.1 A.Displacement of the femoral head relative to the neck through the growth plate B.Idiopathic avascular necrosis due to compromised blood supply to the capital femoral epiphysis C.Infectious destruction of the hip joint D.Inflammatory arthropathy involving the hip joint following a recent viral infection E.Neoplastic infiltration of the femoral metaphysis PathophysiologySubjectMusculoskeletal (Structural disorders)SystemNoneTopic 72.1 A.Biceps tendonitisB.Infraspinatus tendon injuryC.Subacromial bursitisD.Subscapularis tendon injuryE.Supraspinatus tendon injuryF.Teres minor tendon injury AnatomySubjectMusculoskeletal (Shoulder issues)SystemNone

Front: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to progressive left hip and thigh pain for the past 3 weeks. He limps while walking and recently stopped participating in gym class. His mother says he has not had any recent trauma, fever, or known infections. The patient has no history of autoimmune or hematologic disease. He has no urinary symptoms, skin rashes, or weight loss. On physical exam, he walks with an antalgic gait, and passive internal rotation of the left hip elicits pain. His temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 90/min, and BMI is 22.4 kg/m². There is no swelling or erythema of the hip, but the range of motion is reduced. A hip radiograph is obtained and shown. Based on the clinical scenario and radiographic findings, which of the following best explains this patient's condition?
Back:
SCFE typically affects boys aged 10–14, with or without obesity, and presents with gradual-onset limp and limited hip motion. The hallmark radiographic sign is posteroinferior displacement of the femoral head relative to the neck — classically described as “ice cream slipping off the cone.” Prompt recognition and surgical pinning are essential to prevent complications such as avascular necrosis and early osteoarthritis.

Front: A 45-year-old painter presents with 2 months of shoulder pain that worsenswhen lifting his arm overhead or reaching out to the side. The pain is localizedto the lateral shoulder and is most pronounced when he holds his arm out infront at shoulder height with the thumb pointed downward, resistingdownward pressure. He has noticeable weakness during this maneuver butmaintains full passive range of motion. There is no swelling, redness, orhistory of trauma. Strength is preserved in external and internal rotation.Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Back:
Supraspinatus injury is the answer if the patient has impaired shoulder abduction (especially the first 15 degrees) and has a positive empty-can, full-can, and/or drop-arm test. Shoulder pain while lying on one's side in bed or while reaching overhead is classic for both supraspinatus injury and subacromial bursitis. Front: A 17-year-old boy presents to the clinic after injuring his wrist while skateboarding. He reports falling forward and landing on his outstretched right hand, using his palm to break the fall. Since the incident, he has experienced localized pain in the wrist, especially when attempting to grip or twist objects. He denies any numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hand. On examination, there is tenderness over the anatomical snuffbox on the dorsal-radial aspect of the wrist. There is no gross deformity, bruising, or swelling. Range of motion in the wrist is mildly limited due to pain, but he can fully move all fingers. A plain radiograph of the wrist is obtained and interpreted as normal, with labeled bones A through H shown below. Which of the following labeled structures is most likely fractured in this patient?
Back:
Scaphoid fractures are the most common carpal fractures and often present with anatomical snuffbox tenderness following a FOOSH injury. Initial radiographs may be normal; thumb-spica immobilization and follow-up imaging are essential to prevent avascular necrosis.

Front: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician with a 1-week history of rightshoulder pain after lifting weights. Back:
Teres minor is the predominant lateral rotator of the arm when it’s fully abducted.

Front: A 43-year-old carpenter presents to the outpatient clinic due to left shoulder discomfort and difficulty lifting his arm. He reports that the pain began two weeks ago after he spent several hours sanding a ceiling, during which he repeatedly reached overhead. Since then, he has experienced pain when trying to lift objects away from his body and occasionally wakes up at night due to shoulder aching. He denies any numbness, tingling, or neck pain. He has no prior history of trauma or joint dislocation. On physical examination, he holds his left arm close to his body. Passive range of motion is preserved in all directions. However, when asked to actively abduct his left arm, he is only able to initiate a few degrees of movement before experiencing sharp pain over the lateral aspect of his shoulder. The pain worsens when resistance is applied while his arm is held at shoulder height in the “thumbs-down” position. No erythema, swelling, or warmth is noted around the shoulder joint. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's presentation?
Back:
The supraspinatus muscle, innervated by the suprascapular nerve, is responsible for the first 15° of arm abduction. Pain with resisted abduction, especially in the empty-can position, suggests supraspinatus tendonitis or injury. Passive range of motion is preserved in rotator cuff injury, distinguishing it from joint pathology.

Front: A 68-year-old man comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty walking and weakness in his arms and legs over the past two years. He reports that it began as a subtle heaviness in his thighs while climbing stairs but has gradually worsened. Now, he struggles to get up from low chairs and often drops objects when reaching overhead or gripping tools. He denies pain, numbness, or fasciculations. There is no history of trauma, infection, or new medications. He has well-controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. On neurologic examination, he has mild atrophy of the quadriceps and forearm flexors. Strength testing reveals weakness in both proximal and distal muscle groups in the upper and lower extremities. Reflexes are normal and symmetric. Sensation to light touch, vibration, and proprioception is intact. Laboratory studies show normal creatine kinase, electrolytes, and thyroid function. An electromyogram is nonspecific, and the patient undergoes a muscle biopsy for further evaluation. Microscopic examination of muscle tissue reveals scattered basophilic vacuoles bordered by granular material within the myofibers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Back:
Inclusion body myositis is a slowly progressive inflammatory myopathy seen in patients over 50, often with both proximal and distal weakness. Basophilic rimmed vacuoles on biopsy are the diagnostic hallmark. It is often resistant to therapy, distinguishing it from other inflammatory myopathies like polymyositis or dermatomyositis. +

Front: A 46-year-old right-handed woman presents to the clinic with progressive right shoulder pain for the past two months. She reports a dull ache over the lateral aspect of her shoulder that worsens at night, particularly when lying on the affected side. The pain is aggravated by reaching overhead or attempting to put away items on high shelves. She denies any recent trauma, weakness, numbness, or systemic symptoms. She works as a receptionist and has no history of autoimmune or endocrine disorders. Physical examination reveals localized tenderness over the anterior and lateral shoulder with a painful arc between 60° and 120° of active arm elevation. Passive range of motion is preserved in all directions. Strength is normal in elbow flexion, wrist extension, and grip. A plain radiograph of the right shoulder is obtained and shown. Further examination is most likely to reveal impairment of which of the following active shoulder movements?
Back:
Calcific tendinitis of the supraspinatus tendon causes pain during active shoulder abduction due to inflammation and impingement. The supraspinatus initiates the first 15° of abduction, followed by the deltoid and then scapular rotators. Radiographs often show calcium deposits near the greater tuberosity.
